annotations.label: Derivative (instantaneous rate of change)

anchor.snippet: the derivative measures the instantaneous rate at which the value of the function changes

annotations.note: `deriv` is Mathlib's definition of the derivative of a function 𝕜 → F at a point.

annotations.label: Definition of the derivative (limit)

anchor.snippet: the derivative of f at x is commonly denoted by

annotations.note: `HasDerivAt`, characterized by `hasDerivAt_iff_isLittleO`, encodes the limit/difference-quotient definition of the derivative.

annotations.label: Derivative of x squared

anchor.snippet: Thus the slope of the parabola

annotations.note: The derivative of x² is the n=2 special case of the general power rule `deriv_pow`.

annotations.label: Power rule

anchor.snippet: The power rule , for instance, gives

annotations.note: `deriv_pow` states deriv (xⁿ) = n·xⁿ⁻¹, exactly the power rule.

annotations.label: Best linear approximation

anchor.snippet: The derivative also gives the best linear approximation to a differentiable function near a point

annotations.note: `hasFDerivAt_iff_isLittleO` characterizes the derivative as the linear map whose error is little-o, i.e. the best linear approximation.

annotations.label: Interior extremum theorem

anchor.snippet: If a differentiable function has a local maximum or local minimum at an interior point of its domain

annotations.note: `IsLocalExtr.deriv_eq_zero` shows the derivative vanishes at an interior local extremum.

annotations.label: Rolle's theorem

anchor.snippet: Rolle's theorem states that if a function is continuous on a closed interval

annotations.note: `exists_deriv_eq_zero` is Rolle's theorem for a continuous function with equal endpoint values.

annotations.label: Mean value theorem

anchor.snippet: The mean value theorem generalizes Rolle's theorem

annotations.note: `exists_deriv_eq_slope` is Lagrange's mean value theorem.

annotations.label: Zero derivative implies constant

anchor.snippet: if a function has derivative zero throughout an interval, then the function is constant on that interval

annotations.note: `is_const_of_deriv_eq_zero` proves a function with everywhere-zero derivative is constant.

annotations.label: Taylor's theorem

anchor.snippet: Taylor's theorem gives a way to approximate a sufficiently differentiable function by a polynomial

annotations.note: `taylor_mean_remainder_lagrange` gives Taylor's theorem with the Lagrange form of the remainder.

annotations.label: First derivative test

anchor.snippet: The first derivative test uses the sign of the derivative

annotations.note: `isLocalMax_of_deriv`/`isLocalMin_of_deriv` formalize the first derivative test via sign changes of the derivative.

annotations.label: Second derivative test

anchor.snippet: The second derivative test uses the second derivative

annotations.note: `isLocalMin_of_deriv_deriv_pos` (and the neg/max dual) formalize the second derivative test.

annotations.label: Related rates

anchor.snippet: the equation relating them can be differentiated to obtain a relation among their rates of change

annotations.note: Related rates is a problem-solving technique with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Fermat's theorem (stationary points)

anchor.snippet: Fermat's theorem implies that an interior maximum or minimum of a differentiable function can occur only where the derivative is zero

annotations.note: `IsLocalExtr.deriv_eq_zero` is Fermat's stationary-point theorem (same as the interior extremum theorem).

annotations.label: Critical points in several variables

anchor.snippet: A critical point of a scalar-valued function occurs where the gradient is zero

annotations.note: `IsLocalExtr.fderiv_eq_zero` shows the (Fréchet) derivative vanishes at extrema, the multivariable gradient-zero statement.

annotations.label: Geodesic problem

anchor.snippet: Find the shortest curve between two points on a surface

annotations.note: No Mathlib declaration formalizes the geodesic shortest-curve variational problem in this form.

annotations.label: Minimal surface problem

anchor.snippet: Find the smallest area surface filling in a closed curve in space

annotations.note: The minimal surface (Plateau) problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Velocity and acceleration as derivatives

anchor.snippet: velocity is the derivative (with respect to time) of an object's displacement

annotations.note: While `deriv` exists, the physical interpretation of velocity/acceleration as derivatives is not a Mathlib statement.

annotations.label: Velocity and acceleration of a moving object

anchor.snippet: For example, if an object's position on a line is given by

annotations.note: This worked physics example has no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Differential equation

anchor.snippet: A differential equation is a relation between a collection of functions and their derivatives

annotations.note: Mathlib has ODE infrastructure (e.g. `IsPicardLindelof`) but no general definition of a differential equation covering PDEs.

annotations.label: Ordinary differential equation

anchor.snippet: An ordinary differential equation is a differential equation that relates functions of one variable to their derivatives

annotations.note: ODEs are handled via solution existence/uniqueness (`IsPicardLindelof`, `ODE_solution_unique`) rather than a standalone definition.

annotations.label: Partial differential equation

anchor.snippet: A partial differential equation is a differential equation that relates functions of more than one variable to their partial derivatives

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general definition or theory of partial differential equations.

annotations.label: Newton's second law as an ODE

anchor.snippet: Newton's second law , which describes the relationship between acceleration and force

annotations.note: Newton's second law as an ODE is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Heat equation as a PDE

anchor.snippet: The heat equation in one space variable, which describes how heat diffuses through a straight rod

annotations.note: No heat equation declaration exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Zero set

anchor.snippet: This set is called the zero set of f

annotations.note: There is no general 'zero set of a function' definition in analysis (only `zeroLocus` for spectra/polynomials, a different notion).

annotations.label: Implicit function theorem

anchor.snippet: It states that if f is continuously differentiable , then around most points, the zero set of f looks like graphs of functions pasted together

annotations.note: `ImplicitFunctionData.implicitFunction` and `HasStrictFDerivAt.implicitFunction` formalize the implicit function theorem.

annotations.label: Inverse function theorem

anchor.snippet: the inverse function theorem , which states when a function looks like graphs of invertible functions pasted together

annotations.note: `HasStrictFDerivAt.to_localInverse` (with `ContDiffAt.to_localInverse`) formalizes the inverse function theorem for maps with invertible derivative.
